LLM: Quantization reduces model weights from high precision to lower precision (e.g., 4-bit) to save memory and improve efficiency.
One advantage of quantization is reduced GPU memory requirements.
One disadvantage of quantization is a slight reduction in accuracy.
Model quantization is reducing the precision of weights to lower-bit formats.
8-bit quantization offers better accuracy with moderate memory savings.
4-bit quantization saves more memory but may reduce accuracy slightly.
Gemma was developed by Google.
RAG combines document retrieval with a language model to produce more accurate responses.
Retrieval is the process of finding relevant documents based on a query.
ChromaDB stores embeddings and returns the most relevant documents for a query.
RAG can be used in chatbots to provide up-to-date factual answers by retrieving documents.
LoRA is Low-Rank Adaptation, a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method.
The difference between pretraining and fine-tuning: pretraining teaches general language patterns, fine-tuning adapts to specific tasks.
The advantage of LoRA in fine-tuning is that it significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters, making training faster and more efficient.
Embeddings are vector representations of text that capture semantic meaning.
An embedding model converts text into dense numerical vectors that preserve meaning.
Use case: embeddings can be used to search for semantically similar documents.
A vector database stores high-dimensional vectors and allows similarity search.
ChromaDB is a vector database for storing and retrieving embeddings.
LangChain is a framework to build applications powered by language models.
ChromaDB is a vector database for embeddings.
Instruction tuning improves a model's ability to follow human-written instructions.
The benefit of using ChromaDB over a traditional database is that it is optimized for vector similarity search.
The advantage of multilingual embeddings is enabling cross-language semantic search and retrieval.
Embeddings transform text into vectors so that semantically similar queries and documents are close in vector space.
A large language model is a large neural network trained on massive text corpora to understand and generate language.
Knowledge distillation is transferring knowledge from a large teacher model to a smaller student model.
Knowledge grounding in LLMs means connecting model outputs to reliable external data sources for factual accuracy.
Perplexity measures how well a language model predicts text.
A token is a unit of text, such as a word or subword, used by language models.
Context window is the maximum amount of text the model can process at once.
Instruction-response data is a dataset of commands and their expected outputs.
Context length determines how much text the model can consider at once, affecting reasoning and memory.
General ML & DL Concepts: Overfitting happens when a model memorizes the training data instead of generalizing.
Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to minimize loss functions.
Backpropagation is the algorithm used to update weights in a neural network.
Softmax converts logits into probabilities that sum to 1.
Supervised learning is training a model with labeled input-output pairs.
Unsupervised learning is training without labeled data, focusing on discovering patterns.
Reinforcement learning trains models based on rewards and penalties.
An optimizer updates model parameters to minimize the loss function.
A dataset is a structured collection of data used for training or evaluation.
Inference is the process of using a trained model to make predictions on new data.
Few-shot learning is when a model learns to perform tasks from only a few labeled examples.
Evaluation metrics provide quantitative ways to measure how well a model performs on specific tasks.
The role of an optimizer like Adam is to adjust model weights efficiently using adaptive learning rates to minimize the loss function.
A transformer is a neural network architecture based on self-attention mechanisms.
Pruning removes unnecessary weights or neurons to make a model smaller and faster.
An epoch is one full pass through the entire training dataset.
Dropout randomly deactivates neurons during training to reduce overfitting.
Batch gradient descent updates weights using the entire dataset, while stochastic gradient descent updates per sample.
The vanishing gradient problem occurs when gradients shrink too much, hindering deep network training.
A learning rate scheduler dynamically changes the learning rate to improve training convergence.
Multilingual means supporting multiple languages.
Zero-shot learning is the ability of a model to perform tasks without direct training examples.
Parameter-efficient fine-tuning updates only a small subset of parameters, reducing computation and memory costs.
Evaluation measures the performance of a trained model on test data.
Model checkpointing is saving intermediate versions of a model during training.
Early stopping halts training when performance on validation data stops improving.
Fine-tuning is adapting a pre-trained model to a smaller, task-specific dataset.
We fine-tune models to adapt general models to specific tasks or domains.
Transfer learning is reusing a pre-trained model for a new but related task.
The difference between a checkpoint and a final model is that a checkpoint is an intermediate saved state, while the final model is the best-performing or last version.
Computer Vision: Convolution in CNNs applies filters to input images to detect features such as edges or textures.
CNN filters detect visual patterns like edges, corners, or textures in images.
Max pooling reduces the spatial dimensions of feature maps by keeping only the maximum value in each region.
Feature extraction transforms raw images into useful representations for recognition or classification.
Image preprocessing improves image quality and model performance by resizing, normalizing, or denoising images.
Image classification is the task of predicting the category of an image from a predefined set of classes.
Object detection identifies and localizes objects within an image using bounding boxes.
A bounding box is a rectangle used to localize objects in an image.
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to each pixel in an image.
Instance segmentation distinguishes between individual objects, while semantic segmentation groups all objects of the same class together.
Super-resolution is the process of enhancing the resolution of an image using deep learning models.
Optical flow estimates the motion of objects between consecutive frames in a video.
YOLO (You Only Look Once) is a real-time object detection algorithm.
Non-maximum suppression (NMS) removes overlapping bounding boxes to keep only the most confident detections.
A Vision Transformer (ViT) applies transformer architectures directly to image patches instead of using convolutions.
Transfer learning in computer vision reuses a model trained on a large dataset, such as ImageNet, for a new vision task.
Data augmentation generates variations of training images, such as flips or rotations, to improve generalization.
A confusion matrix is a table that shows the performance of a classification model by comparing predictions with actual labels.
Precision measures the accuracy of positive detections, while recall measures how many true objects were found.
Edge detection highlights object boundaries in an image by detecting intensity changes.
Histogram equalization improves image contrast by spreading out pixel intensity values.
Normalization scales pixel values to a standard range, improving training stability.
Image denoising removes unwanted noise from images for clearer recognition.
Data annotation is labeling images with bounding boxes, masks, or class labels.
Active learning selects the most informative unlabeled samples for annotation.
Panoptic segmentation unifies semantic and instance segmentation by labeling all pixels.
SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) builds a map while localizing the system.
Non-maximum suppression (NMS) removes redundant bounding boxes in object detection.
Generative models in vision can create new images, while discriminative models classify patterns.
Diffusion models generate images by progressively denoising random noise.
Explainability methods such as Grad-CAM show which image regions influence model predictions.
Data Mining: Data mining is the process of discovering patterns, correlations, and insights from large datasets.
Association rule mining finds relationships between variables, often used in market basket analysis.
Support measures how frequently an itemset appears in the dataset.
Confidence measures how often items in Y appear in transactions that contain X.
Lift measures how much more likely two items are to occur together than expected if they were independent.
Clustering groups similar data points together without predefined labels.
Classification assigns data points to predefined categories using labeled data.
Outlier detection identifies data points that deviate significantly from the majority.
Apriori is a classic algorithm for mining frequent itemsets and association rules.
FP-Growth is an efficient algorithm for frequent pattern mining using a tree structure.
K-means partitions data into K clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance.
Hierarchical clustering builds a tree-like structure of nested clusters.
Dimensionality reduction reduces the number of features while preserving important information, often using PCA.
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) transforms data into uncorrelated components to reduce dimensionality.
Feature selection chooses the most relevant attributes to improve model performance and reduce complexity.
Text mining extracts useful patterns, trends, or knowledge from textual data.
Web mining analyzes web content, structure, and usage using data mining techniques.
Sequential pattern mining finds frequent subsequences in ordered data.
Preprocessing cleans, normalizes, and transforms raw data to improve mining results.
Discretization converts continuous attributes into categorical ones for easier analysis.
Supervised data mining uses labeled datasets to train models for classification or regression tasks.
Unsupervised data mining analyzes data without labels to discover hidden patterns or clusters.
Regression models the relationship between variables and predicts continuous outcomes.
Decision trees split data based on attribute values to make predictions.
CRISP-DM is a structured methodology for planning and executing data mining projects.
Anomaly detection identifies unusual patterns or rare events.
Data warehousing is the process of collecting and managing large volumes of structured data for analysis.
OLAP enables fast, multidimensional analysis of data for decision support.
Database: A database is an organized collection of structured information stored electronically and managed by a DBMS.
SQL is the standard language for managing and querying relational databases.
A relational database stores data in tables with rows and columns, and relationships between them are defined using keys.
SQL databases use structured schemas and tables, while NoSQL databases are schema-less and store data in flexible formats.
A primary key uniquely identifies each record in a table.
A foreign key is a field in one table that references the primary key in another table.
Normalization organizes data into tables to reduce redundancy and improve consistency.
Denormalization adds redundancy to a database design to speed up read queries.
An index improves query performance by allowing faster searches on specific columns.
A transaction is a sequence of operations performed as a single logical unit of work, ensuring consistency.
ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.
A stored procedure is a set of SQL statements stored in the database and executed on demand.
A view is a virtual table based on the result of a SQL query.
INNER JOIN returns matching rows in both tables, LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right table.
A composite key is a primary key made up of two or more columns.
A schema defines tables, relationships, and constraints in a database.
A deadlock occurs when two or more transactions block each other by holding locks on resources the other needs.
Sharding splits large databases into smaller, faster, and more manageable parts called shards.
Replication copies and maintains database objects in multiple servers for fault tolerance and performance.
A trigger is a set of SQL statements that automatically executes in response to certain events on a table.
A data warehouse is a centralized repository designed for reporting and analysis of large datasets.
A graph database stores data in nodes and edges, making it efficient for representing relationships.
OLTP systems handle transactional data with frequent updates, OLAP systems support complex queries for analysis.
Database caching stores frequently accessed data in memory to speed up queries.
Query optimization chooses the most efficient way to execute a SQL query.
Clustered index determines the physical order of data in a table, non-clustered index creates a separate structure pointing to the data.
Database migration is the process of changing the schema, structure, or location of a database.
CAP theorem states that a distributed system can only guarantee two of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.
Eventual consistency means that all nodes in a distributed database will become consistent over time, but not necessarily immediately.
